headline: Leavitt's arm overpowers LSU in Boston College's statement road win
body: Boston College's passing attack proved too much for LSU's secondary on Saturday, as Sam Leavitt orchestrated a 354-yard aerial performance to secure a 38-28 victory in Baton Rouge. Leavitt's four touchdown passes and efficient 23-for-38 night gave the Eagles control of the game despite being outrushed by 31 yards, a testament to how thoroughly Boston College executed through the air.

LSU's offensive weapons showed up on the stat sheet—Nussmeier matched Leavitt with four touchdown passes and nearly 270 yards, while Chapman hauled in two scoring strikes. Yet the Tigers' ground game stalled when it mattered most, with McNeil managing only 51 yards on six carries. A crucial first-half fumble LSU couldn't recover became the difference in a game where Boston College's ability to sustain drives through vertical passing wore down the home defense.

Harris emerged as a silent force for the Eagles, collecting 119 receiving yards across five receptions without reaching the end zone, consistently moving the chains and setting up scoring situations Leavitt capitalized on. Boston College's defense, while not dominant statistically, forced LSU into uncomfortable third-and-longs that the Tigers couldn't consistently convert. This road victory signals that the Eagles possess the offensive sophistication to compete in any environment.
turningPoint: Boston College's third-quarter drive that extended to 14 plays, consuming nearly eight minutes and resulting in a touchdown pass to extend the lead to two scores, effectively shifted momentum from LSU's early offensive aggression to sustained Eagle control.

unsungHero.name: Harris
unsungHero.why: While Leavitt earned Player of the Game honors for his touchdown tosses, Harris's 119 receiving yards provided the underneath consistency that kept Boston College's offense moving and prevented LSU from tightening coverage over the middle.
